tip: France is vertical blue-white-red. The Netherlands is horizontal red-white-blue; Luxembourg is the same but longer with a lighter sky-blue; Russia is horizontal white-blue-red.
Belgium is vertical black-yellow-red; Germany is horizontal black-red-gold — different color order and direction.
Chad and Romania are almost identical vertical blue-yellow-red (Chad's blue is a touch darker). Andorra and Moldova use the same bands but add a central coat of arms.
Ireland is green-white-orange (green at the hoist). Côte d'Ivoire is the mirror image — orange-white-green. Italy swaps orange for red: green-white-red.
Indonesia and Monaco are both red-over-white (Monaco's is squarer). Poland is the reverse — white-over-red.
All feature an off-center Scandinavian cross; tell them apart by color: Denmark red/white, Norway red-white-blue cross, Iceland blue-white-red cross, Sweden yellow on blue, Finland blue on white.
Colombia is a plain yellow-blue-red with a double-width yellow band. Ecuador and Venezuela share the palette but add a coat of arms (Ecuador) or an arc of stars (Venezuela).

El Salvador, Honduras and Nicaragua all use blue-white-blue horizontal bands with a central emblem. Honduras adds five blue stars; Nicaragua a triangle with a rainbow; El Salvador a golden coat of arms.
Egypt, Iraq, Syria and Yemen all use red-white-black horizontal bands. Tell them apart by the center: Egypt's golden eagle, Iraq's takbir script, Syria's two green stars, Yemen's plain bands.
Mali is vertical green-yellow-red; Senegal adds a green star in the center; Guinea reverses the order to red-yellow-green.
Slovakia, Slovenia, Croatia and Serbia share white-blue-red Slavic colors, distinguished by their coats of arms — and Serbia stacks the colors red-blue-white.
Argentina is three light-blue/white bands with a golden Sun of May in the center; Uruguay has nine white-and-blue stripes with the sun in a white canton.
